Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty minimal in terms of hours. I'd usually carve out time on weekends and maybe one or two evenings during the week, totaling around seven hours. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other things around the house. With limited resources, I had to be strategic about when and how I studied rather than spending tons of time on it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I didn't have a strict Monday-Wednesday-Friday kind of schedule. When assignments were due or exams were coming up, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks, I might barely touch the course materials. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I managed everything.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was new content posted or when I needed to check on upcoming deadlines. Sometimes I'd get a notification about peer activity or a new video, and that would prompt me to jump on. To be honest, I also logged in just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important since my attendance wasn't always great.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became kind of automatic to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee. I'd scroll through, see what was new, watch some videos if I had time. The peer support aspect helped too—knowing other students were active made me feel like I should be checking in regularly. It became part of my daily routine even if I wasn't always deeply engaged.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most useful for me. As an auditory learner, I absorb information way better when I'm hearing it explained rather than reading dense text. I watched almost 87 minutes worth of videos, and I'd often replay sections to really get the concepts down. The peer interactions were also helpful—bouncing ideas off other students clarified things for me.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums entirely. I never posted anything there because it felt time-consuming and I wasn't sure my contributions would be valuable. I also didn't complete a lot of the assignments—only about a quarter of them—because I was prioritizing exam prep over coursework. Some readings I just skimmed or skipped if I felt the videos covered the same material.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on the exams or if it helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. I also valued anything that was efficient—like a 15-minute video that explained something clearly versus a 50-page reading. With limited time and resources, I had to be selective about where I put my energy.
Q8: Definitely different. Required activities felt like obligations that I had to at least attempt, even if I didn't always complete them fully. Optional stuff? I'd only do it if I was genuinely interested or thought it would help my grade. Most optional activities got pushed aside because I was already stretched thin with the mandatory work and life responsibilities.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio from lectures and listened to them on my phone when I was commuting or doing chores. I also borrowed textbooks from friends since I couldn't afford to buy them all, and I'd study from those without being logged into anything. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be lying in bed listening to downloaded lecture recordings, taking mental notes, really processing the material. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. Or I'd be at a friend's place studying together from their textbook, discussing concepts for hours. The LMS would show me as completely inactive during those productive sessions.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that would be my study material for days or even weeks. I'd re-listen to audio files multiple times without ever logging back in. The initial download was just the starting point—the real studying happened offline where it was more convenient and didn't require internet access.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. My stress levels were pretty high, and even though I wasn't feeling particularly anxious, I just felt worn down. I had competing responsibilities outside of school, and my motivation was honestly pretty low. There were weeks where I'd log in, look at everything I needed to do, and just feel overwhelmed and close the laptop.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS became something I'd avoid. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but sometimes later never came. Confusion about certain topics also made me hesitant to engage—if I didn't understand something, I'd procrastinate on that module rather than struggle through it.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be watching videos but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd finish a 20-minute lecture and realize I hadn't absorbed any of it because I was stressed about other things. Or I'd scroll through the LMS checking boxes mentally but not actually engaging with the content in any meaningful way.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was so low. I knew they were important, but I kept telling myself I'd do better on the exams to compensate. Part of it was low motivation, part of it was feeling like I didn't have the time or energy to do them well. I'd rather skip something than submit garbage work.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act sometimes. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation to log in and engage. Without that pressure, I'd drift. The problem was that my engagement was very spiky—intense activity right before due dates, then nothing for a while after.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep going, but honestly I didn't submit enough work to get much feedback. The exam scores were what really mattered to me. When I saw I was doing well on tests, it reinforced my strategy of focusing on exam prep over assignments. Feedback on the few assignments I did complete didn't change my approach much.
Q18: The course structure was flexible enough that I could kind of choose my own path through it, which was both good and bad. Good because I could focus on what I needed, bad because it was easy to skip things. The peer support intervention actually helped—having other students to connect with made the course feel less isolating and more manageable.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement way easier for me. Being able to study offline was crucial given my situation. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or constant internet access. Also, some of the assignment instructions were confusing, which made me less likely to attempt them.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's exploring the content, trying to understand it, but struggling to translate that into completed assignments. Maybe they're dealing with personal issues, or they're perfectionists who can't submit work they're not proud of. High activity might mean they're trying, even if the output doesn't reflect it.
Q21: That's basically my story. I didn't log in constantly or complete many assignments, but I scored really well on exams and got a Distinction. I studied effectively offline, focused on what mattered for assessments, and it worked. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low learning—it might just mean someone found efficient ways to study outside the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, how long they spend on videos. But they miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't tell if someone's actually learning or just going through the motions. They definitely can't capture studying from borrowed books or downloaded materials.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're thinking about course concepts even when you're not logged in, when you're discussing ideas with peers, when the learning feels relevant to you. It's not about clicking through modules—it's about genuine intellectual investment in what you're studying.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through listening to recordings while doing other things, or the conversations I had with peers outside the platform. It wouldn't capture my exam preparation strategies or the fact that I genuinely understood the material despite low assignment completion. It also wouldn't show the stress I was managing or how the peer support helped me stay connected to the course.
